feedback.text: Hi, I am grateful that somebody is finally trying to stop this wild-West tech industry. It would be important to me that this is not exclusively about software but also hardware manufacturers. In particular, I am thinking of ‘smart home appliances’, such as heating, refrigerators, TVs, etc., where the purchase of the equipment is surreptitiously installed as extortion software (BIOS) and it becomes 100 % unusable unless x-euro is paid for monthly ‘protection allowance’ (monthly subscription fees). And this is the case with devices that were able to use OHNE Abo for some years! See: YouTube Luis Rossman on “Futurehome”. Popular with software: Permanent amendment of EULA:(.. And if you don't agree with one, the software that you have switched off will be switched off. Adobe is so a case, which then also made all cloud data inaccessible! Or Blizzard Entertainment. I have bought more than 1000 games... and no longer have access because I do not want to allow Blizzard to collect from me in her last EULA amendment: Result: Blizzard has given me the property of more than 1000! I can understand the amendment of the EULA due to legislative changes – but NOT to take more rights or collect more data from me! EULAs are treaties! It is not clear to me how to change treaties in a way that is easy to change? Is this compatible with European/German law? I also have another problem with the EULAs. They are sometimes extremely long and it is difficult to get older versions. If I now want to know what exactly has been changed, it is extremely difficult, or not at all, to find out! Manufacturers should be compelled to clearly identify any such small change in an EULA! GNP: EuLA old row 234: ‘old wording’ EULA new line 234: ‘new wording’ EULA added new row 1203981: ‘new condition’ It is an absolute unfortunate that firms (a) can change EULAs as they are elusive (b) they are also allowed to tell us (the clients) that, as with any change, we should be allowed to walk through for hours in order to recognise/imagine the changes. And the last point would be to unsubscribe without clicking. If, in seconds, I can log in with only one click, I should be able to unsubscribe with only one click in a few seconds via a button that is oversized centrally on each page! Try to do so on Facebook.) The option to delete account is not easy at all. You will then be forced to be inactive for 30 days In these 30 days, a permanent email is ploughed off their account. And if you just click on this link, the deletion will be SUBSTANCE and you have to wait again 30 days! ‘One-click’ has nothing more to do. Thank you for your efforts. Greeting HH
